# Conclusions and Future Work

This work developed a reproducible, file-driven mutation-evaluation
harness (the Coding Agent MRT / OPENCODE subagent) and used it to
investigate how LLM-based coding agents detect and repair deterministic
source-code mutations. The repository provides an auditable pipeline for
injecting AST-aware edits, quarantining mutated artifacts, and validating
agent proposals via `pytest`-driven test suites. The Methods and
Experiments chapters together show the approach is practical for
replayable evaluation and useful for generating hypotheses about agent
behavior under perturbation.

## Summary of findings

- The OPENCODE harness produces manifest-backed artifacts that enable
  exact replay and provenance for mutation runs.
- In a compact numerical-integration workload, evaluated agents often
  proposed patches that restored passing tests; this demonstrates the
  potential for automated repair in constrained settings.
- High repair rates on small, well-tested examples do not imply
  generalized robustness: scaling to larger projects, richer
  dependencies, and weaker tests will reveal further failure modes.

## Interpretation vs. research questions

- Detection (RQ1): agents reliably report explicit failure symptoms when
  failures are visible in test output or tracebacks; indirect or silent
  regressions are harder to localize.
- Repair (RQ2): agents can produce test-passing patches in constrained
  environments, but passing tests are an incomplete proxy for semantic
  correctness.
- Effort (RQ3): iterations-to-repair and wallclock time depend on
  mutation difficulty, agent strategy, and test execution cost; reports
  should include distributions and per-operator breakdowns.

## Recommendations and future work

- Scale workloads: run the harness on multiple, larger codebases with
  diverse operator mixes and realistic dependencies.
- Improve reporting: run multiple seeds and report aggregated metrics
  (means, variances, confidence intervals) stratified by mutation class.
- Strengthen validation: complement unit tests with metamorphic checks,
  specification-based tests, or lightweight formal verification where
  feasible to reduce overfitting-to-tests.
- Analyze failures: produce a taxonomy of common agent mistakes to
  guide targeted improvements (e.g., API misuse, control-flow flips,
  and off-by-one errors).
- Reduce evaluation cost: add sandboxing and incremental test-caching to
  enable broader comparative studies across many agents.

## Ethical considerations

Mutation-based evaluation can surface vulnerabilities and unexpected
behaviors. Keep experiments limited to non-production code, accompany
public artifacts with clear usage guidelines, and provide responsible
disclosure channels for any security issues discovered.

## Closing remarks

The Coding Agent MRT establishes a practical, replayable platform for
studying agent robustness to code perturbations. The initial results are
promising and identify concrete next steps to move from illustrative
case studies toward rigorous, generalizable benchmarks for automated
repair and robustness in coding agents.
